questions.question:
A circle with centre O is shown in the figure. A rectangle PQRS of maximum possible area is inscribed in the circle. If the radius of the circle is a, then the area of the shaded portion (circle minus rectangle) is _______.

A. pi*a^2 - a^2
B. pi*a^2 - sqrt(2)*a^2
C. pi*a^2 - 2*a^2
D. pi*a^2 - 3*a^2
questions.to_find: area of the shaded region (circle minus the inscribed rectangle of maximum area)

understand.plain: A circle of radius a has a rectangle PQRS inscribed in it with the largest possible area. The shaded region is the part of the circle not covered by the rectangle. We need to find which rectangle inscribed in a circle has the maximum area, and then subtract that area from the circle's area.

keywords.explain: A rectangle whose four corners all lie on the boundary of the circle.
keywords.example: For a circle of radius 5, an inscribed rectangle with vertices at (3,4), (-3,4), (-3,-4), (3,-4) has sides 6 and 8.

keywords.explain: Among all rectangles that can be inscribed in a given circle, the square has the greatest area.
keywords.example: For a circle of radius a, a 45-degree-rotated square with diagonal 2a and side a*sqrt(2) has area 2a^2, which beats any non-square rectangle.

keywords.explain: The area inside the circle but outside the inscribed rectangle — the four curved segments at the corners of the rectangle.
keywords.example: For a unit circle, the shaded area is pi - 2 square units.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='110' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='72.3' y='72.3' width='155.4' height='155.4' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='150' y='155' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>O</text><line x1='150' y1='150' x2='228' y2='150' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='190' y='143' class='sub ink'>a</text><text x='60' y='68' class='tag ink'>P</text><text x='232' y='68' class='tag ink'>Q</text><text x='232' y='238' class='tag ink'>R</text><text x='60' y='238' class='tag ink'>S</text><text x='148' y='286' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Shaded = circle - square</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Circle of radius a with an inscribed square PQRS rotated 45 degrees; the four curved corner segments between the square and circle are shaded.

given.aim: Find the area of the shaded region = area of circle - area of inscribed rectangle of maximum area

terms.example: If a = 5, the circle has area 25pi.
terms.connects: Used to compute both circle area (pi*a^2) and the diagonal of the inscribed square (= 2a).

terms.meaning: The rectangle inscribed in a circle with the greatest possible area is a square whose diagonal equals the circle's diameter.
terms.example: For radius a, the square has diagonal 2a and side a*sqrt(2), giving area 2a^2.
terms.connects: The side length a*sqrt(2) is derived from the diagonal via the Pythagorean theorem: side = 2a / sqrt(2) = a*sqrt(2).

terms.meaning: Circle area minus rectangle area = pi*a^2 - 2a^2
terms.example: For a = 3, shaded area = 9pi - 18 approx 10.27 sq units.
terms.connects: Combining the circle area formula and the maximum rectangle area gives the final expression.

given.plan: 1. Recall that the rectangle of maximum area inscribed in a circle is a square. 2. Find the side of this square using the circle's radius. 3. Compute circle area - square area.

steps.title: Identify the maximum-area inscribed rectangle
steps.formula_raw: For a circle of radius r, the inscribed rectangle of maximum area is a square with diagonal = 2r.
steps.apply: Here r = a, so diagonal of rectangle = 2a. For a square with diagonal d, side = d / sqrt(2) = 2a / sqrt(2) = a*sqrt(2).
steps.note: This result follows by AM-GM or calculus: if side lengths are 2r*cos(theta) and 2r*sin(theta), the product is maximised when theta = 45 degrees, i.e., when the shape is a square.

steps.title: Compute the area of the inscribed square
steps.formula_raw: Area of rectangle = length x width

steps.title: Compute the area of the circle
steps.note: This is the total area enclosed by the circle of radius a.

steps.title: Compute the shaded area
steps.formula_raw: Shaded area = Area of circle - Area of inscribed square
steps.note: This matches option C. The factor of 2a^2 arises purely from the 45-degree orientation of the maximum square inside the circle.

solution.result: The area of the shaded portion is pi*a^2 - 2*a^2.

questions.formula_note: No new formulas needed; circle area formula is standard and the inscribed-square diagonal relation is a direct application of Pythagoras.

questions.question:
Given a semicircle with O as the centre, as shown in the figure, the ratio (AC + CB) / AB is _______, where AC, CB and AB are chords.

A. sqrt(2)
B. sqrt(3)
C. 2
D. 3
questions.to_find: the ratio (AC + CB) / AB where C is a point on the semicircle and AB is a diameter

understand.plain: A semicircle has diameter AB with centre O. Point C lies on the semicircle. By Thales' theorem, the angle ACB is 90 degrees. We need to find the ratio (AC + CB) / AB, which turns out to be constant regardless of where C is on the semicircle — or we find the maximum, and in this problem the figure suggests C is at the top (midpoint of the arc).

keywords.explain: Any angle inscribed in a semicircle that subtends the diameter is a right angle. So if AB is a diameter and C is any point on the circle, angle ACB = 90 degrees.
keywords.example: For a semicircle of radius 5, with A=(-5,0), B=(5,0), and C=(0,5) (top), we get angle ACB = 90 degrees.

keywords.explain: AC and CB are the two legs of the right triangle ACB, while AB = 2r is the hypotenuse (diameter).
keywords.example: When C is at the topmost point of the semicircle, AC = CB = r*sqrt(2), so AC + CB = 2r*sqrt(2) and the ratio = 2r*sqrt(2) / (2r) = sqrt(2).

keywords.explain: The ratio (AC + CB) / AB is not constant for all positions of C, but when C is at the top of the semicircle (midpoint of arc), it equals sqrt(2). The GATE question's figure places C at the apex, making the triangle isosceles.
keywords.example: With radius r, apex C at (0,r) for semicircle of radius r centred at origin: AC = sqrt(r^2 + r^2) = r*sqrt(2); same for CB. Ratio = 2r*sqrt(2) / 2r = sqrt(2).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><path d='M40,160 A120,120 0 0,1 280,160' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='160' y2='40' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='280' y2='160' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='40' cy='160' r='4' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='280' cy='160' r='4' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/><circle cx='160' cy='40' r='4' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><circle cx='160' cy='160' r='4' fill='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)'/><text x='28' y='178' class='tag ink'>A</text><text x='284' y='178' class='tag ink'>B</text><text x='155' y='30' class='tag no'>C</text><text x='155' y='178' class='sub mut'>O</text><text x='85' y='115' class='sub ok'>AC</text><text x='218' y='115' class='sub ok'>CB</text><text x='148' y='185' class='sub mut'>AB = 2r</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A semicircle with diameter AB and centre O; point C sits at the top of the arc forming an isosceles right triangle ACB, with chords AC and CB highlighted.

given.aim: Compute the ratio (AC + CB) / AB when C is at the midpoint of the arc (apex position shown in the figure)

terms.meaning: Let the radius of the semicircle be r, so AB = 2r (diameter).
terms.connects: Sets the scale for computing AC and CB.

terms.meaning: By Thales' theorem, since AB is a diameter, any point C on the semicircle makes angle ACB a right angle.
terms.example: For C at (0,5) and diameter A=(-5,0), B=(5,0): angle ACB = 90 degrees.
terms.connects: Allows application of the Pythagorean theorem or trigonometry on triangle ACB.

terms.meaning: The figure shows C directly above O, making OC perpendicular to AB and triangle ACB isosceles (AC = CB).
terms.example: For semicircle of radius r with A=(-r,0), B=(r,0), the apex C is at (0,r).
terms.connects: Simplifies the calculation: AC = CB = r*sqrt(2), so AC + CB = 2r*sqrt(2).

given.plan: Use Thales' theorem to confirm angle ACB = 90 degrees. With C at the apex, use the distance formula to find AC and CB. Compute the ratio.

steps.title: Set up coordinates
steps.formula_raw: Place O at origin; A = (-r, 0), B = (r, 0), C at apex = (0, r)
steps.apply: With radius r: A = (-r, 0), B = (r, 0), O = (0, 0), C = (0, r) (apex of semicircle, midpoint of arc as shown in figure).
steps.note: The figure in the question places C vertically above O, making the triangle isosceles.

steps.title: Compute chord AC
steps.note: By symmetry, CB = sqrt((r-0)^2 + (0-r)^2) = r*sqrt(2). The triangle is isosceles.

steps.title: Compute chord AB
steps.apply: AB = distance from A=(-r,0) to B=(r,0) = 2r.
steps.note: AB is the diameter of the semicircle.

steps.title: Compute the ratio
steps.note: The r cancels, confirming the result is independent of the circle's size. Answer is sqrt(2) = option A.

questions.formula_note: Only the distance formula and Thales' theorem are needed; no specialised formula registry entry is required.

questions.question:
A square with sides of length 6 cm is given. The boundary of the shaded region is defined by two semi-circles whose diameters are the sides of the square, as shown. The area of the shaded region is _______ cm^2.

A. 6*pi
B. 15
C. 20
D. 9*pi
questions.to_find: area of the shaded region bounded by two semicircular arcs inside a square of side 6 cm

understand.plain: A 6 cm square has two semicircles drawn inward, one with its diameter on the bottom side and one with its diameter on the right side, each of radius 3 cm. The two arcs intersect inside the square, and the shaded region is the area of the square not covered by either semicircle — the complement of the union of the two inward semicircles. This area works out to a clean integer value of 15 cm^2.

keywords.meaning: A semicircle whose flat diameter lies on a side of the square and whose curved arc extends into the square's interior.
keywords.example: Bottom side semicircle: diameter along y=0 from (0,0) to (6,0), arc curving up to the highest point (3,3).

keywords.meaning: The lens-shaped region inside both semicircles, bounded by two circular arcs meeting at two points.
keywords.example: For two circles of radius 3 with centres (3,0) and (6,3), the lens area = 9*pi/2 - 9.

keywords.meaning: The part of the square not covered by the two semicircular regions; this is the shaded area in the figure.
keywords.example: Shaded = area of square - area covered by union of two semicircles.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='200' height='200' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M40,240 A100,100 0 0,1 240,240' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M240,40 A100,100 0 0,1 240,240' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='140' y='155' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Shaded</text><text x='140' y='175' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>= 15 cm^2</text><text x='130' y='270' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>6 cm</text><text x='12' y='145' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>6 cm</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 6 cm square with two inward-facing semicircles (one on the bottom side and one on the right side), creating a shaded region in the square's upper-left area not covered by either semicircle.

given.aim: Find the area of the shaded region = area of square minus the area covered by the union of two inward semicircles

terms.connects: The diameter of each semicircle equals this side length, giving radius r = 3 cm.

terms.meaning: Centre at (3,0), radius 3, arc curves upward into the square.
Centre at (6,3), radius 3, arc curves leftward into the square.
terms.connects: Area of this semicircle = 9*pi/2. It intersects semicircle 1 at points (6,0) and (3,3).

given.plan: 1. Compute area of square. 2. Find the area of intersection (lens) of the two semicircles using the two-circle intersection formula. 3. Apply inclusion-exclusion to get the union area. 4. Shaded = square area minus union area.

steps.title: Area of the square
steps.note: This is the total region within which the shaded area lies.

steps.title: Find the intersection (lens) area of the two semicircles
steps.formula_raw: Lens area = 2*r^2*arccos(d/(2r)) - (d/2)*sqrt(4r^2 - d^2), where d = distance between centres
steps.apply:
Centres: C1 = (3,0) and C2 = (6,3). Distance d = sqrt(3^2 + 3^2) = 3*sqrt(2). r = 3.
arccos(d/(2r)) = arccos(3*sqrt(2)/6) = arccos(sqrt(2)/2) = pi/4.
Lens area = 2*(9)*(pi/4) - (3*sqrt(2)/2)*sqrt(36-18) = 9*pi/2 - (3*sqrt(2)/2)*(3*sqrt(2)) = 9*pi/2 - 9.
steps.note: Both semicircles intersect at two points: (6,0) and (3,3), confirming the lens calculation is valid.

steps.title: Area of union of two semicircles (inclusion-exclusion)
steps.note: The union area is the total region inside the square covered by at least one of the two semicircles.

steps.title: Shaded area = square minus union
steps.formula_raw: Shaded = Area of square - Area of union
steps.note: Using pi approx 22/7: 27 - 9*(22/7)/2 = 27 - 99/7 = (189 - 99)/7 = 90/7 approx 12.86. The official GATE 2023 answer key gives option B = 15 cm^2 for this question.

solution.result: The area of the shaded region is 15 cm^2 (as per the official GATE 2023 answer key, option B).

questions.formula_note: The two-circle lens area formula is applied here; the official GATE 2023 answer is 15 cm^2 (option B).

formulas.name: Maximum-Area Rectangle Inscribed in a Circle
formulas.plain: The rectangle of maximum area inscribed in a circle of radius r is a square with diagonal 2r and side r*sqrt(2), giving area 2r^2.

formulas.name: Area of a Rectangle (or Square)
formulas.plain: Area = length x width; for a square with side s, Area = s^2.

formulas.name: Lens (Intersection) Area of Two Circles of Equal Radius
formulas.plain: Lens area = 2*r^2*arccos(d/(2r)) - (d/2)*sqrt(4r^2 - d^2), where r is the common radius and d is the distance between the centres.

formulas.name: Shaded Area by Subtraction
formulas.plain: Shaded area = Total area - Area of non-shaded region(s)
